“I don’t see how that’s your business,” you say evenly.

\n\nA moment of silence passes before the armored figure nods. “Fair enough, captain. Whatever brings you here will be between you and the Evermind eventually, I’m sure. She’s the god-queen of this planet; you’d do well not to cross her unless you want to end up another part of the spacer graveyard in orbit.”
“I’m here to take what I can get from this shithole,” you tell her. “Look what the natives did with their world. They clearly lost the right to decide what happens to this planet now.”

\n\nThe armor might not have a mouth, but you swear you can feel the scowl radiating off it. “I see. You’d best watch yourself out there then, Captain. The Evermind, this planet’s god-queen, is an absolute authority here - and she has no priority higher than protecting the Phaedrans from exactly your type of spacer. Keep that up and you’ll find yourself on her bad side, and from there, it’s a short trip to the spacer graveyard in orbit.”
\n\n“I’d like to see her try.”
\n\nShe shrugs. “I’m sure you will before long.”
“I’m just here for the adventure of it! Anything else is just a bonus.”

\n\nSomething like a chuckle escapes the armor’s voice-grill. “I see. Well, take it from me, Captain, there are certainly better worlds for playing space cowboy on. I’d suggest you beat feet while you still can. If not... the Evermind, the planet’s god-queen, is very protective of her subjects. It’s only a matter of time before you end up either on her bad side, or as a guest in her court.”
\n\n“See, that sounds like fun!” you grin. “The court of an alien goddess is exactly the sort of place I want to see out here!”
\n\nSeventeen shakes her head. “Do as you will, Captain. I’m certainly not going to stop you.”

“I’m here looking for one of my company’s probes,” you tell her. “It should have arrived on the planet around when the Warp Gate did. Don’t suppose you’ve seen it just lying around, have you?”\n\nSomething like a chuckle escapes the armor’s voice-grill. “I don’t think the locals would just leave something that precious lying around, no. Thank you for the honesty, though. I wouldn’t have blamed you for keeping company secrets close to your chest.
Your, uh, incredibly large chest. I’m sure it’s easy to hide things in there, isn’t it?

A word of advice, though: this system is very close to the Kalas system, so the gate that was sent here would have been one of the first to reach its destination. Ditto your probe. Would have predated the Glass War, so try looking in pre-war vaults or bunkers, anything hardened. An alien artifact would have been kept secure, right? Perhaps given to the Evermind, this planet’s god-queen.”
\n\nThat’s not bad advice...

“Where’s the rest of your crew?” you ask her.
\n\nSeventeen stands stock still a moment, silent. When she speaks again, it’s slow and quiet. “As far as I know, I’m the only representative of KihaCorp on this planet.”

\n\n“We were deployed in individual dropships around the planet’s equator. There were a dozen of us. I lost contact with the rest of the unit after the Evermind lit up the sky with her anti-air cannons. They may have been attempting to return to the Guardian, and...”
\n\nYou get it.
“What can you tell me about the Evermind - this so-called god-queen?”

\n\n“Off-worlders have had little to no contact with her. As you have no doubt seen, she is... unwelcoming to extrasolar interference in her domain. What I can tell you is that she commands the only organized religion left on Phaedra after the Glass War - the natives believe the rest of their pantheon left them or died during the nuclear exchange. She is regarded as the goddess of technology and wisdom in equal measure, a modification of her pre-war portfolio that was entirely based around progressivism and technological advancement at breakneck speed. Perhaps she learned something about unchecked development when her flock killed nearly three quarters of their population overnight.”
\n\n“That certainly would leave an impression.”

\n\nSeventeen nods slowly. “Yeah, that’s one way of putting it. Either way, she’s real: her cultists can literally speak with her, and she has a temple somewhere in the Radwood where she dwells physically with her inner circle.”
\n\n“Know what species she is?”
\n\n“If she has a species, none of the natives will say. She’s a goddess, beyond such things. Her cultic iconography often depicts her as a dragon, however.”
“When I landed, a local went on about a ‘Glass War.‘ What do you know about it?”

\n\n“Total global nuclear war. It took place roughly a century ago, not long after the Warp Gate arrived in the system. From what I understand, it was fought between two of the planet’s dominant cultures over control of Confederate technology they had discovered during their late industrial age. These appear to have been the predecessors to the hyraxi mechanized nomads and the nuka living in Fission City - though there doesn’t appear to be much cultural continuity between them and their ancestors beyond their religion and some semblance of technology. I would advise against assuming the same self-destructive chaos lives on in their descendants... though admittedly, hard times can make hard people, can’t they?”
\n\n“What do you mean?”

\n\nSeventeen shrugs. “The Evermind is committed to ensuring another Glass War doesn’t happen again, hence why her followers go around collecting as much tech as they can, and keeping anything more advanced than a combustion engine out of the hands of the plebs. Still, reconstruction after the war has been slow, and most of the planet’s population are forced into a life of hunter-gathering, or subsistence farming around the few remaining walled cities. If people with nothing to lose see the opportunity to take what they need, or escape the doldrums of their wretched days with a little pleasure, they will.”
“When my cousin was trying to land here, [rival.heShe] got shot down by some serious hardware in the mountains near that big forest. Know anything about that?”

\n\nThe armored woman nods. “The Curtain of Light, the locals call it. Or the Tungsten Dome. There’s a massive hardlight barrier surrounding the Radwoods, projected from hardened installations all around the Shield Mountains. Keeps the radiation exposure to a minimum, and fosters a miniature hydrosystem inside - so it actually rains in the forest. Those same installations have railgun batteries and skyfalcon missile silos that put together have full coverage of the forest all the way up into atmosphere - nothing bigger than a paper plane’s getting to the Curtain, much less through it, without being obliterated.”
\n\nGnarly. “Any way to disable it?”
\n\n“Do I look like I know?” She shrugs. “But... if I was the Evermind, I would keep the control mechanisms to the Curtain close at hand. Inside the dome.”
“Tell me about yourself, XAA-0017. Like a real name, maybe?”

\n\nThe armored woman plants a hand on her hip. “We should keep things professional, captain. You represent Steele Tech, and I represent Kiha. In the field, I’m identified by my serial number. As for me personally? You could say I’m archetypal of a company employee: I like hotrod ships, overcharged energy weapons, and spunky artificial intelligences.”
\n\nYou remember getting a face full of all three back in orbit. So yeah, archetypal sounds about right.

\n\nShe clicks her tongue, bringing her other hand down to her hip and looking down her nose at you as though she were a queen staring down at an unpleasant bootstain. “How very typical of you mortals, to see a maiden in need of aid and coldly turn her down. \n\nSuch a sudden change... “Who are you?”
\n\nThe gynoid gives you a dangerous glare. “Someone better not trifled with. Go then. Waste your life in the dirt; clearly you aren’t the kind to spend it well.”
\n\nShe huffs and turns, striding back around the rock. You take a step after her, but she’s already gone, vanished into the myriad dark crevasses around you.
\n\nWhat the hell...?

“I could be convinced,” you tell her, “but I’d need some encouragement up front...”

\n\nHer smirk spreads, just a touch, and her draconic tail coils around your [pc.leg]. “That is how you [silly|meat-bags|organics] comport yourself, isn’t it? A favor for a favor, scratch my back and I’ll scratch yours? I understand.”
\n\nSyne takes your hand with a wink. “Follow me, then.”

\n\nYou let the gynoid lead you back around behind some rocks, dropping your kit piece by piece as you go while keeping your eyes firmly glued to her tremendous behind as it sways and jiggles with every dainty step. Her tail lifts invitingly, showing off a silky ring and lipless gryvain pussy already glistening with lubricant.
\n\n“So? How do you want me?”
“Okay,” you say. “I’ll help. Where is this creature?”
\n\nSyne smiles brightly, offering you her hand. “Not far at all. I’ll take you.”

\n\nYou follow the fulsome gynoid through the dunes, eyes inevitably drawn to the sway and jiggle of her bare, pale ass that comes with every step. The way she moves truly is hypnotic, and it certainly makes the trip across the dustbowl feel like it takes just a matter of moments...

\n\nThe pair of you crest a dune, and you find yourself looking down at a crater gouged into the sand on the other side. The sand around it has been burned into blackened glass, and metal and polymer shrapnel has been scattered around a crash site perhaps a hundred yards wide and three hundred long. The starship’s hull has been torn open in a dozen places, some by impact, certainly, but you see several holes have been punched straight through her flanks as well. Mass driver hits from the surface, judging by the angles, one of which went clean through the engines.
\n\n“Rough landing,” you say quietly, looking over the wreckage. “[pc.nmh|Did anyone else make it out?|Don’t think anybody walked away from that one...|Everyone die on impact?]”
\n\n“Aside from me, you mean?” Syne says. “No, no organics survived. And now the bones of the vessel are being picked over by a silicone-devouring creature, growing fat on what remains. Look!”
\n\nSomething shifts in the wreckage. Syne gasps, withdrawing back behind the dune as a shadow stalks out from one of the tears in the hull, sniffing at the wasteland air.
Next

The beast that stalks out is a muscular quadruped six feet tall at the shoulders, with a whiplike tail and a plated, horned head that looks like it could headbutt titanium and come out unscathed. If it has eyes, they’re too beady to see beneath its cranial ridges, but your gaze is drawn more to its slavering fangs and rending claws anyway.
The thing’s definitely related to a New Texan varmint, no way around it. Perhaps the crew was smuggling one?

\n\nThe creature sniffs at the air, whipping its tail behind it as it stalks through the shadows. It steps over something as it walks, compressing the middle with enough weight that the top of another gynoid’s head is lifted from the sand covering it. The same model as Syne, you’d guess, but it’s hard to tell for sure from what little is left of the ‘bot; it’s little more than a horned skeleton, synthetic eyes staring vacantly into the air.

\n\nYou draw your [pc.weapon] and slide down the shipward face of the dune. The scaled monstrosity looks up and bares its fangs, letting out a trilling howl that echoes for miles around. When that doesn’t deter you, though, it raises its horns... and bolts back into the ship’s wreckage, stopping only to grab the ravaged gynoid’s neck in its jaws and drag her along with it.
\n\nShit.

You start rifling around through the crew’s belongings. Luckily their footlockers are bolted to the floor and sturdily made, meaning much of their effects were able to survive the crash. You find the usual assortment of personal items you’d expect from Rushers in the lockers: hair brushes, collars, negligee, spent credit chits, and booze whose bottles most regrettably did not survive impact.

\n\nThis crew was definitely mostly ausar: from the holo-pics to the petplay paraphernalia tucked away, you can’t imagine there were more than one or two other races represented on the Son of Jissara’s crew. Hmm, now that you think of it... every last crewman’s locker you look through has at least one lovely, supple leather collar tucked away in it. Sometimes several.

\n\nSetting that discovery aside... you pick out a keychain from the assorted knick knacks, holding it up to the light. Aside from a rabbit’s foot charm, there’s a dozen old-style tumbler-bumpers on the chain, and a single digital card with no markings on it other than a winking face drawn in marker.
\n\nYou pocket the keychain and move on.
\n\n(Key Item Gained: Crashed Ship Keys)

You tap another button on the captain’s armrest, pulling up an emergency diagnostic of the ship’s systems. Aside from the obvious hull breaches and structural integrity failures, your attention is immediately drawn to an all-caps alert: WARNING: REACTOR BREACH DETECTED. ENGINEERING SEALED. Damn. The automated damage control systems have activated throughout the ship, sealing off the engineering deck’s power reactor from the rest of the Son. Radiation shields have also dropped to protect the crew quarters as well; they would have sealed the bridge, if the door hadn’t been hit by so much shrapnel that the bulkhead couldn’t move down. Lucky you, considering the only other possible way in is buried six feet down.
\n\nFrom here you could choose to end the damage control lockdown, which would get you into engineering at least. And also unleash whatever those bulkheads dropped to protect the crew from...

\n\nAlerts pop up on the holos, telling you that dangerous radiation is spilling out from engineering. Further, because of the radiological alert, the crew quarters are still sealed directly from engineering. You won’t be able to countermand the seal until the reactor breach is dealt with.
\n\nBetter get a move on before the radiation becomes too severe...

blockHeader("Captain’s Log")

You tap the replay button on the command throne’s arm, bringing back the holographic figure of the femboy kitty-captain. It looks like there are still several log entries still stored in the chair’s memory bank, sorted by date.
Back

“Captain’s log, supplemental. It is the eve of the 14th Great Planet Rush, and the start of our grand adventure. The Son of Jissara has been commissioned by the Akkadi Research and Development Group to survey a group of seven select planets as potential points of interest to their science department. As part of this new contract, the Son has undergone extensive refit, giving us vastly improved sensor and scientific suites on par with the most cutting-edge research ships.”

\n\n“And, of course, we’ve taken on new crew as well. Commander Kiara Dorna, formerly of her family’s Warp Field Institute, has left a decidedly very long lasting impression on me already. I look forward to seeing her bonding with the rest of our tight-knit crew over the coming years. In addition, we’ve been joined by crewman M’tera Vhess, formerly a specialist in artificial intelligence at KihaCorp. She’s a beta dzaan, though she certainly has an alpha’s aura in the most delightful ways, and marks the only non-ausar on my crew aside from myself. She came highly recommended, however, including a personal mark of excellence from Kiha herself, so while our own computers expert is out on maternity leave I’m willing to make an exception for this tour.”

\n\n“With the crew back to full strength and our new upgrades installed, we only have to wait until midnight and the activation of the Fourteenth Rush’s new gates. At Commander Dorna’s suggestion, I’ve had a case of Hauâ Maian red brought aboard to toast with. Let’s make Ms. Vhess feel welcome!”

“Captain’s log, R14-point-one-point-forty-two. With crewman Vhess having recovered from her ordeal on DeLuca VII, we’ve charted course for the second new planet we’re to explore as part of our Akkadi contract: Nyphea. Initial scans show it to be an incredibly lush, verdant forested world teeming with fauna, including what appears to be a quadrupedal sapient species. There is no sign of technology on the planet more advanced than the bow and arrow, however, so Akkadi’s interest in the planet is likely to be minimal. Still, in the name of due diligence, we will conduct a thorough scan of the planet from orbit before moving on to Phaedra.”

\n\nThere is a long pause before you hear the kaithrit’s voice again. The hologram shifts to show him reclining in his throne with an ausar woman in his lap, wearing a high heeled version of the same jumpsuit, though zipped down to the navel. It, too, has a conspicuous bulge. “Captain’s log, supplemental. Severe atmospheric interference in the equatorial jungles forced the crew to make landfall to complete our analysis of the planet, and inevitably we have made first contact with the primitive tauric natives. The native race, the dhrisa, have proven... remarkably accommodating to my fully female crew, but staunchly refused to so much as speak to myself, Commander Dorna, or crewman Vhess. The ladies were given the honor of participating in a native religious ritual ongoing at the time of our arrival, dedicated to their goddess of fertility. Communications with our away team are out, leaving the three of us back aboard the Son with little to do but get to know each other a little better...”

\n\nAnother pause. This time the kaithrit’s wearing a supple leather collar and a short sarong, both beautifully stitched with floral patterns. “Captain’s log, supplemental. The away team has returned from their cultural exchange in high spirits and bearing gifts. I have been invited down to the surface to meet with their high priestess now that the crew has uncovered the cultural faux-pas we stumbled into. Apparently ‘breeders’ - that is, those of us with breeding poles between our legs - are not to be so much as acknowledged in public without a collar on to denote which ‘seed-bearer’ we belong to, nor are we allowed to wear any clothing that conceals our privates. Doctor Gyse has offered to be my, ah, chaperone down to the surface. She insists that I will find the meeting of great interest, and won’t take no for an answer.”

“Captain’s log, R14-point-one-point-fifty-two. Commander Dorna discovered a curative to the pheromonal mind control the natives had used to take control of the away team. She and crewman Vhess led a rescue mission to the surface that ended my week long captivity in the high priestess’s harem. During the ensuing battle, we discovered a subterranean chamber beneath the fertility temple that housed a starship of unknown provenance. The ship was ancient, carbon dated to at least a thousand years ago. Crewman Vhess was able to program an artificial intelligence to brute force the ship’s security, and we’ve downloaded its memory banks for Akkadi analysis once we return to port. At first glance, the ship appears to be incredibly advanced, possibly overmatching anything in the Confederate arsenal, but we’ll be saving detailed investigations for the corporate research unit. With any luck, they’ll pay a bonus bounty for the pheromonal countermeasures Commander Dorna developed.”

\n\nA pause, and the captain smirks. “In the meantime, Kiara has also synthesized the dhrisan pheromones into a cologne. It appears to put fertile women into a suggestible but sexually aggressive state. Initial research shows that, when mixed with masculine sex pheromones, the cologne makes you nearly irresistible... whether you like it or not. By way of apology for their behavior, the ladies have all agreed to a few days of rigorous field testing before we move on to our next survey world, Phaedra.”

\n\nThe recording hitches, and the captain reappears wearing a collar and sarong, looking utterly disheveled and exhausted. “Captain’s log... is very fucking sore. Let the record show that women are not the only ones affected by the cologne. Kiara, uh, Commander Dorna... is suffering from acute dehydration and fatigue. Ship’s lube supply: virtually exhausted. Condom supply: utterly depleted. Doctor Gyse: "+(isSillyModeEnabled()?"submissive AND breedable":"definitely pregnant")+". I’m going to lock the remaining supply of cologne down in her name, before Kiara wakes back up and ends up with another crewman knotted for six hours. At least if it’s not me. End log.”

“Captain’s log, R14-point-one-point-fifty-five. Having finished our survey of Nyphea, one of the most beautiful garden worlds I’ve ever seen, the Son of Jissara has just arrived in orbit of Phaedra II. The contrast is striking. Initial scans indicate that this world has suffered a prolonged radiological event, possibly indicative of nuclear war. While we detect humanoid life signs, they are scattered and number only in the tens of millions across the planet, and there is little evidence of advanced technology on the surface. We have, however, detected numerous satellites in orbit, suggesting the natives at one point at least achieved a primitive space age. We have received no response from the surface to any hails. In order to fulfill our mission, we’ll have to do things the hard way...”

“Captain’s log, R14-point-one-point-fifty-seven. We have made contact with several small tribes living in the wastelands of Phaedra using our emergency backup radios - an excellent suggestion on the part of crewman Vhess. It appears that the natives are recovering from a total nuclear exchange some hundred years ago, which wiped out their society and reduced their developmental progress to a state of, to quote Doctor Gyse, ‘techno-barbarism.‘ Despite Vhess’s objections, we’ve decided to make landfall near the home of a particularly tech-savvy tribe of hyraxi, a species superficially similar to the ausar, to attempt to trade our food and medical supplies for local technology so we can begin our analysis.”

\n\nThe captain grins and leans back in his chair, steepling his fingers. “From what we’ve exchanged with the hyraxi tribe, I have at least one thing in common with them: a love of jetbikes. Perhaps there’s something in the inventory I can trade with them for one. We’ll be landing momentarily!”

\n\nThere’s a fritz in the hologram, and suddenly the captain’s been replaced by a dzaan woman so thick that she’s visibly struggling to fit into the command throne. There’s a massive bulge in her jumpsuit sitting heavy over her pressed-together thighs. She’s got several techno-gadgets strapped to her belt and arms, including a holo-bracer she’s quickly typing on.

\n\n“Chief systems operator’s log, supplemental,” she says hurriedly. “While Captain Leoric and the others are engaging in trade with the Phaedran natives, I’ve confirmed my theory regarding extranet ‘ghost’ signals on the planet’s surface. Someone, or something, is running a dark Q-COMM network separated from the public net. There’s no way to breach it without a hardwire connection to a port, but the reverse isn’t necessarily true. We’ve got so many Q-COMM devices running in the science department alone, it’s like hanging out a ‘please hack me’ sign to anybody who knows their way around EWS. There’s already evidence that our flight systems have been probed by an outside source. I need to tell the captain before it’s too late!”
\n\nShe heaves herself up and goes running, and the recording ends.

\n\nThe pain only lasts a moment, before darkness consumes you.

The blue-scaled monstrosity crumples, giving one last plaintive whine before it falls still. The deck rumbles under the weight of the creature’s collapse, and then... silence falls. You keep your weapon leveled at the beast a moment longer until you’re sure it’s down for good before finally relaxing, turning your attention back towards the console on the wall.
\n\nEven if that threat’s been dealt with, you’re still soaking up an unhealthy amount of radiation"+(pc.hasAirtightSuit()?" and your sealed armor isn’t going to protect your forever":"")+".

“I’ll be, you actually made it back!” the gynoid muses, rising from her hiding place behind the sand-swept dune. “I really do know how to pick ‘em. So, did you get the core?”

\n\nYou nod, fishing the silicone sphere out of your pack. Syne’s eyes light up, quite literally, as she closes the distance between you. Her milk-white fingers brush the surface of the orb, and a faint smile spreads across her dark lips. “Thank you, captain. You have no idea what this means to me.”

It occurs to you what you learned is on the Son of Jissara’s computer core: all the information about the ancient starship its crew discovered. You hesitate in handing the core over to Syne, mind racing with possibilities.

\n\n“Something on your mind, captain?” Syne asks innocently, leaning in pressing her shoulders together in a way that heaves up her bosom. “Perhaps thinking about the way I’m going to show my gratitude for your help? Because I’m very grateful for what you’ve done for me, risking your very life for a stranger!”

“Hello there, stranger. You don’t look like you’re from around here.”

\n\nYou choke off a shocked gasp, glancing to your side to the source of the voice. Somehow you didn’t see her passing by that rock, but there’s a woman leaned against it in the shade, watching you with a bemused smirk on her blood-red lips. For a split second you almost mistake her for
Olympia - as if your engineer would ever leave her beloved starship
that draconic engineer-bot you merc’d back on the pirate station
Olympia, somehow having followed you all the way to Phaedra
- but once the shock of her arrival wears off, you’re able to see that there’s more than a few subtle differences.

\n\nThe gryvain-gynoid’s skin is milk pale"+(void 0!==flags.MET_GIANNA?", just like Gianna’s":"")+", though she has the same burgundy scales, and her midriff is adorned by a faintly luminescent tattoo of a winged ouroboros. Glittering hardlight jewelry hangs from her horns, as do a pair of large nipple rings that sway with every simulated breath. Her smirk spreads, and a dainty hand meanders over a boob and down her soft middle, drawing your eye from weighty bosom to the equine cock-sheath and fat black balls resting heavy between her thick thighs.
\n\n“Neither do you,” you finally manage to respond, tearing your eyes off the unexpected showing of all that naked synth-flesh. “What’s a companion A.I. doing all the way out here?”

\n\n“Same thing you are, I should think,” she says. “Exploring. Learning. Entertaining the locals. Trying not to get irreparably irradiated... which is not a small ask on this planet, as I’m sure you’ve noticed. Right now, though: I’m watching you.”
\n\nYou glance around, suddenly wary. “"+(pc.isMischievous()?"So... like what you see?":"And you’re doing that because...?")+"”

\n\nThe gynoid pushes herself up off the stone, taking an easy stride towards you. Every step does jaw-dropping things to her ass, breasts, and balls, all suddenly a-sway as she moves. <i>“Take it easy, Captain, I’m not going to hurt you.
At least, not if you don’t give me a reason to.
You seem like a reasonable sort, after all. Not like most of the wastelanders.
Mmm. I was actually wondering if you’d do me a little favor?”
\n\n“[pc.nmh|I can certainly give it a try,|Well I guess that depends!|We just meet and you’re already asking for handouts?]” you say. “What is it?”

\n\n“Mmm. I was the property of a Confederate citizen, you see, but I crash-landed on this planet a while ago. I’ve... been by myself ever since. It’s been hard. The locals have taken a liking to me, for reasons I’m sure you can guess at,” she smiles, flicking one of her hardlight rings. “But there are certain things I can’t ask them to do. Exploring a crashed KihaCorp dropship being one of them.”
\n\nArching a brow, you say, “You haven’t even told me your name yet.”

\n\nA delicate giggle answers you, utterly incongruous with the sinfully sexual nude body circling you. “Aha, my apologies, Captain. Like I said, I’ve been on my own for a long time. I’m, well, you can call me Syne. A silicone-eating specimen of radtwisted fauna has inhabited the wreckage, and would positively shred this armature of mine if I got close. I just need you to slip inside and pull the computer core for me. I don’t have any credits, not like they’re much use down here anyway, but I’m entirely confident I can make it worth your while. What do you say?”
\n\nShe gives you a broad smirk, one hand on a egg-layer’s hip and the other still looping fingers through her nipple ring.

You’re standing at the edge of the dunes overlooking the downed starship. Shards of polymer and metal stick out of the ground like new buds. To the east, the sand has been burned into a blackened glass by the flaming impact of the crash. You can just make out an airlock on that flank of the hull too, its door long since fallen away.\n\nSyne is hiding behind the dune, watching you intently.

The ground underfoot here is nothing but blackened glass, super-heated by the ship’s reentry. Every step sends crick-cracks echoing through the ravine.
\n\nThe dune Syne’s hiding behind is back west; the ship’s airlock is east.

The devastated starship is within arm’s reach from here, canted slightly to starboard and sitting in a field of black glass and shrapnel. You can make out part of the ship’s name still stenciled across the hull: “S N  OF J  S RA,” accompanied by an icon that’s far too burnt to identify. The airlock sits to the north of where you stand, open to the elements now that its door has been peeled away; the dunes stretch back to the west.

The airlock’s open, allowing you to walk between the dunes and the interior of the stricken ship at-will. Everything out here smells burnt, and there’s a melted quality to the walls and consoles - you suppose the outer door must have come off during reentry.

As you step into the crashed ship’s airlock, a robotic voice intones from a melted console beside the outer door: “War...ning... hull... breeeeech... war...ning...” Yeah, no kidding. You slap the cycle button, and watch with a grimace as the inner door simply falls inward when its motors try and actuate. The ship shudders, echo rocking through the sundered hull. “War...ning... rad...eeeeeeee...ation... aleerrrrrrrrtttttttt. Fuel... leek... Fuel... leek... war...ning...” That doesn’t sound good. Better make this quick.

You’re standing in the heart of the downed starship, what once was the crew’s living space. The furniture and appliances have been thrown this way and that by the impact, but you can see a leather sofa, a fridge, and a couple of card tables amidst the wreckage. The stove took a railgun hit directly; there’s not much of it left aside from an outline in the counter and a lot of mechanical bits strewn around.

\n\nIt looks like the crew’s quarters are north, "+(1!==flags.KIHAN_GYNOID_QUEST_OPENED_STOREROOM?"though the door has been sealed by a blast shield, ":"")+"and the bridge is to the west. South leads back to the airlock, and east deeper into the ship.

The crew’s quarters are divided up military style: a single high cabin for the captain roomed off from the rest of the crew, who live in a round chamber with bunk beds lining the walls and footlockers beneath them.

\n\nThe captain’s quarters took a direct hit from a missile, leaving little left of it besides a bulged door and a few scraps of personal belongings that didn’t get sucked into space. The captain’s desk has crumpled into the impact hole despite its metallic construction, somewhat plugging the breach.
\n\nThere’s a communal shower and commode, with something rusty-hued dripping from the pipes.

You step onto the bridge, carefully planting your [pc.feet] as the ship’s prow takes a steep downwards angle. The forward viewscreen’s been shattered, letting in hundreds of pounds of sand from where the front of the ship plowed under the ground. From the angle, it looks like the nosedive broke the ship’s back. She’ll never fly again if that’s true. Shame.

\n\nYou take a few cautious steps forward, running your hand over the back of the captain’s chair - though perhaps “throne” would be more appropriate. It’s covered in gilded ancient ausari hieroglyphs, like it was looted out of some ancient Star King’s tomb. Hell, maybe it was. It certainly survived the impact better than anything else on the bridge. Your fingers come across a button on the armrest, and a hologram flickers to life in the chair, a ghostly echo of the man that might once have sat it. He’s a boyishly handsome kaithrit lad in a tight bodysuit that hugs an even tighter bulge showing between the legs casually flung over the arm of his chair as he speaks.

\n\n“Captain’s log, R14-point-one-point-fifty-five. Having finished our survey of Nyphea, one of the most beautiful garden worlds I’ve ever seen, the Son of Jissara has just arrived in orbit of Phaedra II. The contrast is striking. Initial scans indicate that this world has suffered a prolonged radiological event, possibly indicative of nuclear war. While we detect humanoid life signs, they are scattered and number only in the tens of millions across the planet, and there is little evidence of advanced technology on the surface. We have, however, detected numerous satellites in orbit, suggesting the natives at one point at least achieved a primitive space age. We have received no response from the surface to any hails. In order to fulfill our mission, we’ll have to do things the hard way...”
\n\nThe hologram flickers and cuts out.
\n\nAt least you’ve got a name for the ship, now...

The helm and weapons posts are ahead of the captain’s chair and several tactical assessment consoles surround the dias from behind.

\n\nThe main viewscreen’s been shattered in the crash, letting in loads of sand and dirt from the wastes; the prow must be buried half a dozen feet underground for how much muck it’s scooped into the bridge. The ship’s science labs run the length of the ship between its main living area back west and the engineering section at the eastern rear. There’s a tremendous amount of sensitive equipment here, all utterly destroyed by the crash. Microscopes, test tubes, and analysis devices of every kind are smashed against the walls or crushed beneath furniture. The only thing that’s survived is a travel computer inside a hardened briefcase. The thing’s come open and the screen is cracked, but it’s still functional enough to show that it was in the process of some kind of radiological analysis. You don’t have the degrees necessary to understand what all those numbers mean, but you guess all the red is bad news.

The science deck narrows near the rear end, allowing a second deck to run underneath it on the flanks. The ship’s escape pods are docked here, as well as the damage control equipment the crew might need to fight fires or attend to serious injuries. None of the pods have been jettisoned.
\n\nThe console for the ship’s automated damage control system has been smashed, leaving it inoperable.
\n\nThe science deck is back west, and the engineering section is just east of here.

The main engineering area sits through a bulkhead at the back of the ship, dominated by a massive power reactor that stretches down into a lower deck. You slide down the ladder and duck under the half-raised emergency shielding that separated the reactor core from the rest of the ship. “Anno, take a look a this.” You take the strange transceiver device out and set it on the snowy pup’s desk.

\n\nHer tail does one quick wag as she looks the device over, chewing on the end of her pen and humming to herself. She holds it up to the light, twisting it this way and that to observe every inch before finally taking out a little toolkit from her drawer and prying it open. A couple of sparks fly when she scrapes some still-powered part inside, but that just makes her flinch and not much else. She grumbles and pokes around a little more, glancing between it and you a couple times.
\n\n“Hmmm. Yep, this sure is a model seven local net Q-Comm transceiver. What, um, what am I supposed to be looking for here?”
\n\nYou lift a brow. “Model seven?”

\n\n“Yeah?” Anno leans back to her terminal and taps a couple buttons, bringing up an extranet page. A holo-model of the exact same part you gave her pops up, orbiting her display while a red and white dragon silently breathes fire in the background. “This was one of KihaCorp’s best sellers back in my parents’ days. I think dad’s got one in his hunting pack for emergencies.”
\n\n“You’re sure?” you ask her, taking the device back and holding it up to the holo. It’s definitely the same part, down to the wire. “Because I found this on a rusty old war robot on Phaedra.”

\n\nAnno quirks her head aside. “Well, that’s... weird, considering KihaCorp wasn’t even founded until after the 13th Rush. And, well, Phaedra’s in the 14th Rush zone, so, I’m not sure how they’d have any Confederate products in their ‘bots a hundred years ago. It couldn’t have come with the Warp Gates. Maybe someone with access to modern tech was refurbishing it? Another survivor from the first exploration group?”
\n\n“Maybe...”

\n\nYour science-pup shrugs. “Dunno what else to tell you, short of contacting Kiha customer support and looking up the serial num...ber... which this one doesn’t have.” She peers at the bottom of the transceiver. “Look, there’s the molding for the sticker to go on, but no trace of it.”
\n\nYou sigh. “Great. Thanks for the help, Anno.”
\n\nShe snaps a crisp salute. “Any time, captain!”

“Hey, Olympia, what do you make of this?” you ask, tossing the Phaedran device to your gynoid engineer.

\n\nShe glances at it for a split second before returning to her work. “This is a KihaCorp model seven local quantum network transceiver. As advertised, it can connect devices up to one thousand kilometers away without need of relay, or can allow for global network access with a monthly subscription. It currently has a manufacturer’s suggested retail price of one hundred and ninety-nine credits. It is however six models outdated, so of no particular interest.”
\n\n“Except that it was in a century-old Phaedran war robot.”

\n\nOlympia pauses, glancing at the part again. “That is... unlikely, Captain. This model was an early product of KihaCorp, which was founded after the 14th Planet Rush’s Warp Gates were dispatched. Meaning there is no way it could have gotten to Phaedra before the planet’s nuclear exchange short of a wormhole or time travel. And scans have shown no evidence of either temporal or spatial anomalies in the system.”
\n\n“I know where I got this,” you tell her.

\n\nShe shrugs. “Be that as it may, the timeline doesn’t add up. My body was made by KihaCorp, and still has an extensive product catalog loaded in hard memory; there can be no mistake about the object’s identity. However, a brief spectrum analysis does indicate the object is roughly as old as KihaCorp is - it was likely one of the first produced by the company in this product line. And... it has significant nuclear isotopic residue, which at least suggests it was in close proximity to an atomic detonation. I would suggest contacting KihaCorp to look up the serial number, but it seems this particular example was never given one.”
